title: Pest control product cost recovery – Climate, nature and economy lens public statement - Canada.ca
content:
## Pest control product cost recovery – Climate, nature and economy lens public statement
By charging fees to registrants, Health Canada ensures that the burden of funding pesticide regulatory activities is shared between taxpayers and registrants of pest control products. Health Canada reviewed its pest control product annual charge in 2023 and found that fees do not reflect current regulatory costs, with taxpayers assuming increased economic burden. For example, compliance and enforcement costs are funded entirely from public funds.
To address this issue, proposed amendments to the Pest Control Products Fees and Charges Regulations would replace the current sales-based system for determining annual charges with a tiered charge, based on the number of pest control product registrations held by the registrant. To mitigate the effect of the increase to the annual charge, and to replace the fee reductions being removed, the proposed amendments would also provide a reduced annual charge. The following reduced annual charges in the proposal are most likely to have potential environmental impacts:
- pest control products that are, or contain as their only active ingredient, semiochemicals, microbial agents, or non-conventional active ingredients would have a reduced annual charge of $1000 per registration, regardless of the size of business holding the registration; and,
- certain pest control products that are considered specialized or "niche" products, regardless of the size of business holding the registration, would also have a reduced charge of $1000 per registration.
A detailed analysis was triggered only for environmental impacts of this proposal, as regulatory proposals are exempt from economic impact analysis requirements.
This proposal is likely to have indirect positive impacts on biodiversity and the environment. However, the magnitude of the impacts are likely to not be significant.
One of the main objectives of this proposal is to increase the sustainability of the pesticide regulatory system, particularly for post-market activities such as re-evaluation, through higher cost recovery from registrants . This increased cost recovery will contribute positively to meeting Goal 12 of the Federal Sustainable Development Strategy (FSDS), namely, to reduce waste and transition to zero-emission vehicles. This increase in the pesticide regulatory system's sustainability will directly support the "continue review of pesticides" aspects of the implementation strategy.
Moreover, registrants may be more willing to submit or retain registrations for semiochemical, microbial, non-conventional, or niche products as a result of the reduced annual charge situations noted above. The increased or retained registrations for such products may assist Canada in meeting Goal 15 of the FSDS, which aims to protect and recover species and conserve Canadian biodiversity.
Internationally, the proposed amendments are expected to assist Canada in meeting two targets of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework:
- Target 6, which seeks to reduce impacts on biodiversity due to invasive alien species; and,
- Target 7, which seeks to reduce the overall risk from pesticides and highly hazardous chemicals.
The proposed amendments assist in meeting these two targets because they would provide a financial incentive to submit or maintain registrations for pest control products that, for example, are for the management of a situation involving an invasive species or tend to have lower environmental risk profiles than "conventional" pest control products.
The proposed amendments would contribute to establishing a more appropriate sharing of costs between public and private contributions for the post-market oversight of pest control products. A more appropriate sharing of costs would, in turn, strengthen regulatory oversight of pest control products and increase the sustainability of post-market activities, such as re-evaluations, which in turn would lead to increased resilience and adaptation to current and future climate change conditions.
